romania.title: Recordul nebunesc stabilit de doi aventurieri cu o călătorie de 22.500 km într-un „coșciug cu roți”
romania.summary: Britanicul Ollie Jenks își amintește de momentul în care prietenul său i-a propus pentru prima dată ideea. „Era atât de ridicolă încât nu puteam spune nu”, a declarat Jenks pentru The Associated Press. Propunerea venită de la prietenul său canadian Seth Scott, la rândul lui pasionat de mașini și de aventuri nebunești, era ca ei …

Duminică. 26 Aprilie 2026, la Sala Auditorium (MNAR).… Articolul Haendel continuă primăvara barocă a Stagiunii de Muzică Veche București apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: O fetiță de 2 ani a murit în ambulanță, după ce i s-a făcut rău, în Constanța. Poliția a deschis o anchetă
romania.summary: Poliţiştii au deschis o anchetă după ce o fată de un an şi nouă luni din judeţul Constanţa a murit, după ce a început să se simtă rău, iar mama a decis să o transporte cu maşina la medicul de familie. Pe drum, starea fetiţei s-a agravat, iar femeia a sunat la 112 şi pe drum s-a întâlnit cu o ambulanţă. Echipajul medical a început manevrele de resuscitare, dar fără rezultat.

romania.title: Valori interpretative pe „Drumul spre celebritate”
romania.summary: Valori interpretative pe „Drumul spre celebritate” Asociația Pro Valores, Radio România – prin Orchestrele și Corurile Radio, Radio România Cultural și Uniunea de Creație Interpretativă a Muzicienilor din România lansează cea… Articolul Valori interpretative pe „Drumul spre celebritate” apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: DevTalks România ajunge, în 2026, la cea de-a 13-a ediție, confirmând fără echivoc statutul său de cel mai așteptat eveniment de technologie din România și din Europa Centrală și de… Articolul Cea mai mare conferință de tehnologie din România și CEE are loc pe 3–4 iunie la Romexpo, B1 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: 😂 Wizz Air anunţă că nu scumpeşte biletele, dar le va cere pasagerilor câte 10 litri de kerosen la check-in
romania.summary: Veşti bune pentru pasagerii companiilor aeriene low-cost! Wizz Air a anunţat că va păstra neschimbate preţurile biletelor pentru majoritatea destinaţiilor, impunându-le călătorilor o singură condiţie: să aducă la check-in un bidon, butoiaş, găleată sau damigeană cu 10 litri de kerosen. „La întoarcere, pasagerii îşi vor putea recupera bidonul de la acelaşi birou de check-in, iar […] The post Wizz Air anunţă că nu scumpeşte biletele, dar le va cere pasagerilor câte 10 litri de kerosen la check-in appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: O nouă fabrică digitală Siemens în România: cu emisii zero de carbon și cu procese bazate pe transferul de date
romania.summary: O nouă fabrică Siemens, digitalizată și bazată pe inteligență artificială, va fi construită la Sibiu până în 2028, după obținerea avizelor necesare, pe baza unei decizii strategice de investiție pentru… Articolul O nouă fabrică digitală Siemens în România: cu emisii zero de carbon și cu procese bazate pe transferul de date apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Pentru a păstra structura coaliției de guvernare intactă chiar și după ce PSD-ul a decis să iasă de la guvernare, premierul a bătut palma cu Omul cu Pesediști, astfel că vom avea în continuare pesediști în Guvern. Datorită acestei magistrale strategii puse în practică de Ilie Bolojan, „noua” […] The post Coaliția poate continua. Ilie Bolojan a bătut palma cu Omul cu Pesediști appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Ca să îl vadă mai multă lume, coiful de la Coţofeneşti va fi expus pe Stegarul Dac
romania.summary: După sperietura trasă cu jaful din Olanda, statul român e hotărât să îşi pună mai mult în valoare patrimoniul arheologic! Astfel, Muzeul de Istorie a decis să afişeze Coiful de la Coţofeneşti într-un loc public, pentru ca acesta să poată fi admirat gratuit de cât mai mulţi oameni. „Am hotărât să expunem coiful pe Stegarul […] The post Ca să îl vadă mai multă lume, coiful de la Coţofeneşti va fi expus pe Stegarul Dac appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
romania.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
romania.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

romania.source: Hope and Homes for Children Romania
romania.title: „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj
romania.summary: Hope and Homes for Children, alături de Distribuție Oltenia, parte din Evryo, a derulat campania „Grijă pentru mine”, un proiect dedicat sănătății emoționale aadolescentelor din medii vulnerabile. Inițiativa a ajuns la peste 250 de fete din județul Gorj, inclusiv tinere din sistemul de protecție și din comunități rurale, oferindu-le sprijin pentru a-și înțelege emoțiile, a-șicrește... Articolul „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj apare prima dată în Hope and Homes for Children .

romania.title: De la un proiect Teach for Romania la o inițiativă europeană: cum elevii din Reghin construiesc „Leadership HUB”
romania.summary: O idee pornită din dorința de a dezvolta competențele de leadership ale elevilor s-a transformat, în doar câțiva ani, într-o inițiativă educațională care conectează școli din mai multe țări europene. Povestea proiectului „Leadership HUB” începe cu un proiect finanțat de Teach for Romania și continuă astăzi prin colaborări internaționale în cadrul platformei eTwinning și al […]

medical.title: Move smarter not harder: How less exercise is more
medical.summary: If you think you need to "go hard" at the gym to make your muscles stronger, think again. New research at ECU has revealed strenuous exercise and soreness is not needed to improve muscle size, strength, or performance.

medical.title: Severe strokes linked to 5 times higher dementia risk
medical.summary: As stroke severity increases, the risk of progressive cognitive decline and dementia substantially escalates, according to a national study led by Michigan Medicine researchers. People with the most severe strokes had five times higher odds of developing dementia and showed cognitive decline equivalent to being more than two years older at baseline compared with people who did not have a stroke. The results are published in JAMA Network Open.

medical.title: Voting is linked to living longer
medical.summary: A new study co-authored by Femida Handy of the School of Social Policy & Practice at the University of Pennsylvania finds that, among older adults, voting predicts a lower risk of mortality for up to 15 years.

medical.title: Dual-pathway blockade therapy offers hope for rare, hard-to-treat gynecological clear cell cancers
medical.summary: Before her cancer diagnosis in early 2017, 48-year-old Caitlin Delaney, who is based in Australia, had been struggling with a troubling mix of symptoms comprising extreme fatigue, chronic sinusitis, and appetite changes including a sudden aversion to foods such as chocolate and alcohol. Detailed tests and scans eventually revealed the cause—an advanced stage of clear cell ovarian cancer. For another patient, Michelle (not her real name), aged 49, the road to diagnosis came in late 2022 after months of persistent acid reflux, bloating, and ongoing indigestion, which similarly pointed to the same cancer—clear cell carcinoma of the ovary.

medical.title: Two-faced protein discovery may explain why leukemia drugs fail or succeed
medical.summary: A KAIST research team has identified the real reason why anticancer drugs kill cancer cells—targeted anticancer therapies do not simply block cancer proteins but rather shut down the "protein factories" inside the cells, forcing them to undergo self-destruction. Consequently, the "two-faced protein" that plays a key role in this process is gaining attention as a breakthrough for treating patients with drug resistance.

medical.title: Finding the best dietary supplements for cycling performance—and recovery
medical.summary: Focusing on the rigors of elite cycling, Flinders University experts have put performance-enhancing and other dietary supplements under the microscope, rating some more highly than others. From carbs, beetroot juice and the latest sports gels, the new research highlights how a range of dietary supplements may help improve cycling performance, working to support the body's energy systems and reducing fatigue during exercise.

medical.title: TAVR Google searches surge over 300% in a decade: Corresponds with expanded adoption of transcatheter approaches
medical.summary: New research found that public interest in Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement (TAVR) increased substantially over the last decade, with Google search volume rising by 340% from 2015 to 2025, while searches for SAVR (Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement) declined by 42%. These trends parallel a shift in practice, as clinicians doubled TAVR use between 2015 and 2021.

medical.title: Using a new online mental health tool just once boosts UK teenagers' hope and emotional well-being
medical.summary: Research finds that one-time use of a free online tool can positively support young people's mental health and well-being. Developed by psychologists and health researchers at the University of Bath, Project Action Brings Change (ABC) UK is a free digital support tool—known as a digital single‑session intervention (SSI)—that provides short, accessible and evidence-based psychological support and well-being activities.

medical.title: 3D DNA switch in brown fat could reshape obesity and diabetes treatment
medical.summary: Most fat stores energy; the body's brown fat does the opposite. Unlike the white fat that accumulates just under our skin, brown fat burns calories and glucose to generate heat. Formally known as brown adipose tissue, it is a specialized metabolic tissue whose importance in adults has only come into focus in recent years. Brown fat—stored in small, distinct pockets deep within the body—plays an outsized role in body-weight regulation and blood-sugar control, making it an attractive target for metabolic therapies.

medical.title: Eating more fruits and vegetables tied to unexpected lung cancer risk
medical.summary: A surprising new study suggests that eating a very healthy diet—packed with fruits, vegetables, and whole grains—might be linked to a higher risk of lung cancer in younger non-smokers. Researchers found that patients under 50 diagnosed with lung cancer often had better-than-average diets, raising the possibility that pesticide exposure from conventionally grown produce could be a hidden culprit.

science.title: Do polysaccharide-degrading enzymes also help build polysaccharides?
science.summary: Most of the carbon fixed by plants through photosynthesis is ultimately stored in the cell wall, primarily in the form of polysaccharides such as cellulose, xylan, and glucomannan. Yet how plants efficiently synthesize these wall polymers has remained unclear, particularly because polysaccharides such as glucomannan and xylan are prone to aggregation through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions.

science.title: Brazil unearths a bizarre beaked reptile with a trans-Atlantic prehistoric link
science.summary: Paleontologists from the Federal University of Santa Maria (UFSM) have published a new study in the scientific journal Royal Society Open Science, in which they describe a new species based on a fossil skull approximately 230 million years old. The specimen was discovered within the Quarta Colônia UNESCO Global Geopark, in southern Brazil, at a fossil site that has already yielded some of the oldest dinosaurs in the world.

science.title: Molecular chains unlock atomically precise nanoribbons for next-generation electronics
science.summary: Scientists have developed a unique way to build electronic components so small they are made from chains of individual molecules—creating a toolbox to help build materials that could power the next generation of technology.

science.title: Mozambique 'sky island' expeditions found four new species of chameleon that are already at risk from forest loss
science.summary: Tropical rainforests are known for their unique biodiversity, with species found nowhere else on Earth. But nearly 30% of tropical rainforest has been destroyed or has become seriously degraded since 1990. Many of these forests have not been fully explored for their biodiversity. This means that the world may be losing species before they are even discovered by modern science.

science.title: Machine learning helps detect roars from lion collars without recording actual audio
science.summary: Roaring over long distances is a key behavior of lions. They communicate within prides as well as with other animals using distinct sequences of moans and grunts. Scientists from the GAIA Initiative have now published a machine learning approach in the journal Ecological Informatics that improves how roaring behavior can be studied.

science.title: What wild honey from the Philippine jungle reveals about biodiversity
science.summary: In the Philippines, Indigenous communities have been harvesting wild honey for centuries. A new chemical analysis of this honey now provides insights into the biodiversity of the region. "And an additional reason to protect the national tree properly," says lecturer Merlijn van Weerd of the Centre for Environmental Sciences (CML).

science.title: Only some kinds of job losses cause voters to elect strong leaders, study finds
science.summary: Americans are more likely to turn to authoritarian leaders when jobs are being lost due to offshoring than they are when job losses result from automation, an international research team has found. This is despite the fact that automation tends to lead to greater job losses.

science.title: Why groups slowly stop working well together, even when conditions are good
science.summary: Humans are generally a cooperative bunch and most of us probably like to think of ourselves as reliable team players. Cooperation is useful for all sorts of reasons, from running a business and managing community resources to supporting our neighbors. But cooperation is fragile and it slowly starts to fizzle out even under favorable conditions, according to a new study published in Nature.

science.title: What's that swirly pattern? It's a moiré, and it has potential power
science.summary: Just as wave-like patterns can appear on a computer screen when pixels do not align, new research led by Flinders University is investigating atomic-scale "moiré patterns" in the promising field of ferroelectricity. The new study, with experts at Monash University and Nanyang Technological University in Singapore, seeks inroads into electrical and optical science by exploring these complex "superlattice" patterns in various ways to create new energy and material capabilities.

science.title: 3I/ATLAS contains 30 times more semi-heavy water than comets in our solar system
science.summary: New observations of the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS include the first measurement of the abundance of deuterated water relative to ordinary water in an interstellar object. Astronomers using the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) discovered that the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS is made of an astonishingly high ratio of semi-heavy water relative to water, indicating that its system of origin likely formed under conditions far colder than our own. The findings are published in Nature Astronomy.

science.title: Scraped from ancient Roman toilets, these crusted remains expose a pathogen found far earlier than expected
science.summary: Modern analytical tools are no less than a time machine. From their 21st-century labs, researchers can peer into the everyday lives, hygiene, and even the parasites that plagued the people who lived centuries ago. In one such recent venture, a team from Poland turned their attention to the Roman province of Moesia Inferior, studying the dried remains of human waste preserved in Roman toilets dating from the 2nd to 4th centuries CE.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Recordul nebunesc stabilit de doi aventurieri cu o călătorie de 22.500 km într-un „coșciug cu roți”
mix_items.summary: Britanicul Ollie Jenks își amintește de momentul în care prietenul său i-a propus pentru prima dată ideea. „Era atât de ridicolă încât nu puteam spune nu”, a declarat Jenks pentru The Associated Press. Propunerea venită de la prietenul său canadian Seth Scott, la rândul lui pasionat de mașini și de aventuri nebunești, era ca ei …

mix_items.title: O nouă fabrică digitală Siemens în România: cu emisii zero de carbon și cu procese bazate pe transferul de date
mix_items.summary: O nouă fabrică Siemens, digitalizată și bazată pe inteligență artificială, va fi construită la Sibiu până în 2028, după obținerea avizelor necesare, pe baza unei decizii strategice de investiție pentru… Articolul O nouă fabrică digitală Siemens în România: cu emisii zero de carbon și cu procese bazate pe transferul de date apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Wizz Air anunţă că nu scumpeşte biletele, dar le va cere pasagerilor câte 10 litri de kerosen la check-in
mix_items.summary: Veşti bune pentru pasagerii companiilor aeriene low-cost! Wizz Air a anunţat că va păstra neschimbate preţurile biletelor pentru majoritatea destinaţiilor, impunându-le călătorilor o singură condiţie: să aducă la check-in un bidon, butoiaş, găleată sau damigeană cu 10 litri de kerosen. „La întoarcere, pasagerii îşi vor putea recupera bidonul de la acelaşi birou de check-in, iar […] The post Wizz Air anunţă că nu scumpeşte biletele, dar le va cere pasagerilor câte 10 litri de kerosen la check-in appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Ca să îl vadă mai multă lume, coiful de la Coţofeneşti va fi expus pe Stegarul Dac
mix_items.summary: După sperietura trasă cu jaful din Olanda, statul român e hotărât să îşi pună mai mult în valoare patrimoniul arheologic! Astfel, Muzeul de Istorie a decis să afişeze Coiful de la Coţofeneşti într-un loc public, pentru ca acesta să poată fi admirat gratuit de cât mai mulţi oameni. „Am hotărât să expunem coiful pe Stegarul […] The post Ca să îl vadă mai multă lume, coiful de la Coţofeneşti va fi expus pe Stegarul Dac appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: O fetiță de 2 ani a murit în ambulanță, după ce i s-a făcut rău, în Constanța. Poliția a deschis o anchetă
mix_items.summary: Poliţiştii au deschis o anchetă după ce o fată de un an şi nouă luni din judeţul Constanţa a murit, după ce a început să se simtă rău, iar mama a decis să o transporte cu maşina la medicul de familie. Pe drum, starea fetiţei s-a agravat, iar femeia a sunat la 112 şi pe drum s-a întâlnit cu o ambulanţă. Echipajul medical a început manevrele de resuscitare, dar fără rezultat.

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Do polysaccharide-degrading enzymes also help build polysaccharides?
mix_items.summary: Most of the carbon fixed by plants through photosynthesis is ultimately stored in the cell wall, primarily in the form of polysaccharides such as cellulose, xylan, and glucomannan. Yet how plants efficiently synthesize these wall polymers has remained unclear, particularly because polysaccharides such as glucomannan and xylan are prone to aggregation through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions.

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: Brazil unearths a bizarre beaked reptile with a trans-Atlantic prehistoric link
mix_items.summary: Paleontologists from the Federal University of Santa Maria (UFSM) have published a new study in the scientific journal Royal Society Open Science, in which they describe a new species based on a fossil skull approximately 230 million years old. The specimen was discovered within the Quarta Colônia UNESCO Global Geopark, in southern Brazil, at a fossil site that has already yielded some of the oldest dinosaurs in the world.

mix_items.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
mix_items.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Molecular chains unlock atomically precise nanoribbons for next-generation electronics
mix_items.summary: Scientists have developed a unique way to build electronic components so small they are made from chains of individual molecules—creating a toolbox to help build materials that could power the next generation of technology.

mix_items.source: Hope and Homes for Children Romania
mix_items.title: „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj
mix_items.summary: Hope and Homes for Children, alături de Distribuție Oltenia, parte din Evryo, a derulat campania „Grijă pentru mine”, un proiect dedicat sănătății emoționale aadolescentelor din medii vulnerabile. Inițiativa a ajuns la peste 250 de fete din județul Gorj, inclusiv tinere din sistemul de protecție și din comunități rurale, oferindu-le sprijin pentru a-și înțelege emoțiile, a-șicrește... Articolul „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj apare prima dată în Hope and Homes for Children .

mix_items.title: De la un proiect Teach for Romania la o inițiativă europeană: cum elevii din Reghin construiesc „Leadership HUB”
mix_items.summary: O idee pornită din dorința de a dezvolta competențele de leadership ale elevilor s-a transformat, în doar câțiva ani, într-o inițiativă educațională care conectează școli din mai multe țări europene. Povestea proiectului „Leadership HUB” începe cu un proiect finanțat de Teach for Romania și continuă astăzi prin colaborări internaționale în cadrul platformei eTwinning și al […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique 'sky island' expeditions found four new species of chameleon that are already at risk from forest loss
mix_items.summary: Tropical rainforests are known for their unique biodiversity, with species found nowhere else on Earth. But nearly 30% of tropical rainforest has been destroyed or has become seriously degraded since 1990. Many of these forests have not been fully explored for their biodiversity. This means that the world may be losing species before they are even discovered by modern science.

mix_items.title: Machine learning helps detect roars from lion collars without recording actual audio
mix_items.summary: Roaring over long distances is a key behavior of lions. They communicate within prides as well as with other animals using distinct sequences of moans and grunts. Scientists from the GAIA Initiative have now published a machine learning approach in the journal Ecological Informatics that improves how roaring behavior can be studied.

mix_items.title: What wild honey from the Philippine jungle reveals about biodiversity
mix_items.summary: In the Philippines, Indigenous communities have been harvesting wild honey for centuries. A new chemical analysis of this honey now provides insights into the biodiversity of the region. "And an additional reason to protect the national tree properly," says lecturer Merlijn van Weerd of the Centre for Environmental Sciences (CML).

mix_items.title: Only some kinds of job losses cause voters to elect strong leaders, study finds
mix_items.summary: Americans are more likely to turn to authoritarian leaders when jobs are being lost due to offshoring than they are when job losses result from automation, an international research team has found. This is despite the fact that automation tends to lead to greater job losses.

mix_items.title: Why groups slowly stop working well together, even when conditions are good
mix_items.summary: Humans are generally a cooperative bunch and most of us probably like to think of ourselves as reliable team players. Cooperation is useful for all sorts of reasons, from running a business and managing community resources to supporting our neighbors. But cooperation is fragile and it slowly starts to fizzle out even under favorable conditions, according to a new study published in Nature.

mix_items.title: What's that swirly pattern? It's a moiré, and it has potential power
mix_items.summary: Just as wave-like patterns can appear on a computer screen when pixels do not align, new research led by Flinders University is investigating atomic-scale "moiré patterns" in the promising field of ferroelectricity. The new study, with experts at Monash University and Nanyang Technological University in Singapore, seeks inroads into electrical and optical science by exploring these complex "superlattice" patterns in various ways to create new energy and material capabilities.

mix_items.title: 3I/ATLAS contains 30 times more semi-heavy water than comets in our solar system
mix_items.summary: New observations of the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS include the first measurement of the abundance of deuterated water relative to ordinary water in an interstellar object. Astronomers using the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) discovered that the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS is made of an astonishingly high ratio of semi-heavy water relative to water, indicating that its system of origin likely formed under conditions far colder than our own. The findings are published in Nature Astronomy.

mix_items.title: Scraped from ancient Roman toilets, these crusted remains expose a pathogen found far earlier than expected
mix_items.summary: Modern analytical tools are no less than a time machine. From their 21st-century labs, researchers can peer into the everyday lives, hygiene, and even the parasites that plagued the people who lived centuries ago. In one such recent venture, a team from Poland turned their attention to the Roman province of Moesia Inferior, studying the dried remains of human waste preserved in Roman toilets dating from the 2nd to 4th centuries CE.

mix_items.title: Move smarter not harder: How less exercise is more
mix_items.summary: If you think you need to "go hard" at the gym to make your muscles stronger, think again. New research at ECU has revealed strenuous exercise and soreness is not needed to improve muscle size, strength, or performance.

mix_items.title: Severe strokes linked to 5 times higher dementia risk
mix_items.summary: As stroke severity increases, the risk of progressive cognitive decline and dementia substantially escalates, according to a national study led by Michigan Medicine researchers. People with the most severe strokes had five times higher odds of developing dementia and showed cognitive decline equivalent to being more than two years older at baseline compared with people who did not have a stroke. The results are published in JAMA Network Open.

mix_items.title: Voting is linked to living longer
mix_items.summary: A new study co-authored by Femida Handy of the School of Social Policy & Practice at the University of Pennsylvania finds that, among older adults, voting predicts a lower risk of mortality for up to 15 years.

mix_items.title: Dual-pathway blockade therapy offers hope for rare, hard-to-treat gynecological clear cell cancers
mix_items.summary: Before her cancer diagnosis in early 2017, 48-year-old Caitlin Delaney, who is based in Australia, had been struggling with a troubling mix of symptoms comprising extreme fatigue, chronic sinusitis, and appetite changes including a sudden aversion to foods such as chocolate and alcohol. Detailed tests and scans eventually revealed the cause—an advanced stage of clear cell ovarian cancer. For another patient, Michelle (not her real name), aged 49, the road to diagnosis came in late 2022 after months of persistent acid reflux, bloating, and ongoing indigestion, which similarly pointed to the same cancer—clear cell carcinoma of the ovary.

mix_items.title: Two-faced protein discovery may explain why leukemia drugs fail or succeed
mix_items.summary: A KAIST research team has identified the real reason why anticancer drugs kill cancer cells—targeted anticancer therapies do not simply block cancer proteins but rather shut down the "protein factories" inside the cells, forcing them to undergo self-destruction. Consequently, the "two-faced protein" that plays a key role in this process is gaining attention as a breakthrough for treating patients with drug resistance.

mix_items.title: Finding the best dietary supplements for cycling performance—and recovery
mix_items.summary: Focusing on the rigors of elite cycling, Flinders University experts have put performance-enhancing and other dietary supplements under the microscope, rating some more highly than others. From carbs, beetroot juice and the latest sports gels, the new research highlights how a range of dietary supplements may help improve cycling performance, working to support the body's energy systems and reducing fatigue during exercise.

mix_items.title: TAVR Google searches surge over 300% in a decade: Corresponds with expanded adoption of transcatheter approaches
mix_items.summary: New research found that public interest in Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement (TAVR) increased substantially over the last decade, with Google search volume rising by 340% from 2015 to 2025, while searches for SAVR (Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement) declined by 42%. These trends parallel a shift in practice, as clinicians doubled TAVR use between 2015 and 2021.

mix_items.title: Using a new online mental health tool just once boosts UK teenagers' hope and emotional well-being
mix_items.summary: Research finds that one-time use of a free online tool can positively support young people's mental health and well-being. Developed by psychologists and health researchers at the University of Bath, Project Action Brings Change (ABC) UK is a free digital support tool—known as a digital single‑session intervention (SSI)—that provides short, accessible and evidence-based psychological support and well-being activities.

mix_items.title: 3D DNA switch in brown fat could reshape obesity and diabetes treatment
mix_items.summary: Most fat stores energy; the body's brown fat does the opposite. Unlike the white fat that accumulates just under our skin, brown fat burns calories and glucose to generate heat. Formally known as brown adipose tissue, it is a specialized metabolic tissue whose importance in adults has only come into focus in recent years. Brown fat—stored in small, distinct pockets deep within the body—plays an outsized role in body-weight regulation and blood-sugar control, making it an attractive target for metabolic therapies.

mix_items.title: Eating more fruits and vegetables tied to unexpected lung cancer risk
mix_items.summary: A surprising new study suggests that eating a very healthy diet—packed with fruits, vegetables, and whole grains—might be linked to a higher risk of lung cancer in younger non-smokers. Researchers found that patients under 50 diagnosed with lung cancer often had better-than-average diets, raising the possibility that pesticide exposure from conventionally grown produce could be a hidden culprit.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Am zis ca fac sport. Am facut: am fugit de responsabilitati.

satire_ro.title: Wizz Air anunţă că nu scumpeşte biletele, dar le va cere pasagerilor câte 10 litri de kerosen la check-in
